title: PTSD — Session 9 (Trauma-Focused CBT)
label: PTSD (Trauma-Focused)

goals: Process traumatic memories using TF-CBT framework
Develop trauma narrative with reduced affective flooding

text:
Therapist: How are you coming in today? You mentioned over text that last week was harder.

Patient: Yeah. I had a really bad Tuesday. I was at the grocery store and a man in line behind me had a beard similar to — similar to his. And I just froze. I couldn't check out. I left my cart and went to my car and sat there for 40 minutes.

Therapist: That sounds like a really intense experience. Can you walk me through what was happening in your body when you froze?

Patient: My heart was pounding. I couldn't breathe properly. Everything felt like it was happening in slow motion and loud at the same time. I thought I was going to pass out.

Therapist: That's a classic trauma response — what we call hyperactivation. Your nervous system identified a threat cue — the beard — and shifted into survival mode even though you were completely safe. That's not a failure, that's a trauma-trained brain doing exactly what it learned to do. How long did the flooding take to subside?

Patient: Like 20 minutes? And then I felt completely wiped out for the rest of the day. Couldn't do anything.

Therapist: That exhaustion afterward is called the post-activation collapse. It's physiologically real. Your body expended a lot of adrenaline. I want to acknowledge how much courage it takes even to go to the grocery store given what you're carrying. You didn't flee the situation entirely — you stayed in the car until it passed. That's window of tolerance work.

Patient: It didn't feel like courage. It felt like failure. I keep thinking I should be better by now.

Therapist: Nine sessions is not a long time for what you've been through. Trauma doesn't follow a straight line. Let's look at where you were at session one — you couldn't drive past that street, remember? You're driving now. You're shopping now. The beach trip last month — that happened.

Patient: True. I did do all of that. The beach was actually okay.

Therapist: More than okay — you told me it was the first time you'd felt present in months. That's data about your capacity. Today I want to continue with the trauma narrative work. We left off before we got to the apartment. Are you willing to go there today?

Patient: I think so. I want to get through it. I'm so tired of it running my life.

Therapist: That motivation is important. Remember the tools — you can signal with your hand if you need to pause. We'll use grounding if the window closes. Let's also check your present-moment anchor before we begin. Can you feel your feet on the floor right now?

Patient: Yes.

Therapist: Good. Eyes open or closed, whatever feels safer. When you're ready, take me back...

[Trauma narrative work — 25 minutes — patient verbally processed the incident with appropriate pacing, two brief grounding pauses, significant affective processing but remained within window of tolerance. No dissociation observed.]

Therapist: You did really important work today. I want to check in — where are you right now on a scale of 0 to 10?

Patient: Like a 4. Actually a 4. Which is lower than I expected to feel.

Therapist: That's meaningful. The work is working. Before we close, I want to add one grounding practice to your week — the 5-4-3-2-1 technique after any triggering event. I'll send you the worksheet. And don't white-knuckle through grocery stores alone if you can avoid it — bring someone for the next few weeks while we consolidate this.

Patient: My sister said she'd come with me.

Therapist: Perfect. Lean on that support. You're not doing this alone.
